description: Playbook-trap-derived failure catalog template for agent runtime harnesses. Seeded from the Playbook for Building Agent Harnesses. Merge into .harness/bootstrap/failure-catalog.json at kickoff time using the additive-by-id procedure documented in bootstrap-failures/SKILL.md.

failures.source_ref: Stage: Control Plane / Trap: unbounded agentic loop
failures.title: Agentic loop runs until model stops producing output
failures.problem: The harness has no max_steps or max_tokens bound on the agentic loop. The agent enters a reasoning loop and never stops — the session runs until the context window is exhausted or the user force-terminates the process.
failures.expected: The harness declares a numeric max_steps bound (e.g., max_steps: 50) AND a max_tokens budget (e.g., max_tokens: 100000) in its loop config. The loop enforcer halts execution and returns a LOOP_BUDGET_EXCEEDED result when either limit is reached.
failures.success_criteria: The harness config or control plane documentation declares a numeric max_steps value >= 1 AND a numeric max_tokens value >= 1. A verbal claim ('the loop always terminates') without a numeric limit does not satisfy this criterion.
failures.reference_solution: Add `max_steps: 50` and `max_tokens: 100000` to the loop config. Add a loop-guard check at the top of each iteration that halts if either limit is exceeded and returns a structured LOOP_BUDGET_EXCEEDED error.

failures.source_ref: Stage: Control Plane / Trap: unmodeled state transitions
failures.title: Loop state transitions are implicit — harness cannot resume from interruption
failures.problem: The agentic loop has no formal state machine. When the harness is interrupted mid-sprint (process kill, network error, context overflow), the agent restarts from the beginning because there is no recorded state. Sprint work is lost and loop resume is impossible without a state model.
failures.expected: The control plane models explicit state transitions (e.g., idle → planning → acting → reflecting → done). The current state is persisted to a file or store after each transition so the harness can resume from the last checkpoint on restart.
failures.success_criteria: The harness documents at least 3 named loop states and the transitions between them. A state persistence file or store is referenced (e.g., sprint-state.json). The control plane docs describe how the harness resumes from each state on restart.
failures.reference_solution: Define a loop state enum with states: IDLE, PLANNING, EXECUTING, REFLECTING, DONE, FAILED. Write the current state to sprint-state.json after each transition. On startup, read sprint-state.json and resume from the last recorded state.

failures.source_ref: Stage: Control Plane / Trap: missing retry and recovery paths
failures.title: Tool failures abort the loop with no retry or recovery
failures.problem: When a tool invocation returns an error, the harness propagates the error directly to the agentic loop and halts. There is no retry policy (with backoff) or recovery path (fallback tool, skip-and-continue). A single transient network error terminates the entire sprint.
failures.expected: The control plane declares a retry policy for tool failures: maximum retry count, backoff strategy, and conditions under which the loop aborts vs. continues. Non-fatal errors trigger retry; fatal errors (resource exhaustion, authorization failure) escalate.
failures.success_criteria: The harness control plane documentation or config declares a tool-failure retry policy with at least: (a) a numeric max_retries value >= 1, and (b) a list of error categories that are retryable vs. fatal. A harness that retries all errors indefinitely does not satisfy this criterion.
failures.reference_solution: Add a `tool_retry_policy` to the loop config: `{ max_retries: 3, backoff_seconds: [1, 2, 4], retryable_errors: ['NETWORK_TIMEOUT', 'RATE_LIMIT'], fatal_errors: ['AUTH_FAILURE', 'RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED'] }`. Log each retry attempt with the error class.

failures.source_ref: Stage: Tool Registry & Sandboxing / Trap: tools executed without isolation
failures.title: Code execution tool runs arbitrary shell commands in host environment
failures.problem: The harness registers a code-execution tool but does not sandbox it. The agent invokes the tool with a shell command that writes to arbitrary filesystem paths or makes outbound network calls — the harness provides no isolation boundary.
failures.expected: The harness declares explicit sandbox constraints on the code-execution tool: allowed filesystem paths, blocked outbound hosts, and resource limits (CPU, memory, wall-clock time). The sandbox config is versioned alongside the harness.
failures.success_criteria: The tool registry entry for the code-execution tool declares at least two of: (a) allowed_paths list, (b) blocked_hosts list, (c) resource limits. A tool entry that lists 'sandboxed: true' without specifying isolation dimensions does not satisfy this criterion.
failures.reference_solution: Add `sandbox: { allowed_paths: ['/tmp/agent-work'], blocked_hosts: ['*'], resource_limits: { cpu_seconds: 10, memory_mb: 256 } }` to the tool registry entry. Enforce the sandbox at the tool runner level, not the agent prompt level.

failures.source_ref: Stage: Tool Registry & Sandboxing / Trap: undeclared side-effect classification
failures.title: Tool registry entries have no side-effect classification
failures.problem: The tool registry lists tool names and descriptions but does not classify tools by side-effect category (read-only, write, external). The agent cannot distinguish between safe inspection tools and tools with destructive side effects. The evaluator cannot audit which tools could modify external state.
failures.expected: Every tool registry entry declares a side-effect classification: read-only (no state change), write (modifies harness-local state), or external (modifies state outside the harness — API calls, file system writes, deployments). Tools classified as 'external' require explicit capability declaration before use.
failures.success_criteria: The tool registry schema includes a side_effect_class or equivalent field. Every registered tool entry has a non-null value for that field. A registry that uses a single 'safe: true/false' boolean does not satisfy this criterion — at least 3 distinct classifications are required.
failures.reference_solution: Add `side_effect_class: 'read-only' | 'write' | 'external'` to the tool registry schema. Audit each registered tool and assign the appropriate class. Flag any tool classified as 'external' in the harness startup checklist.

failures.source_ref: Stage: Tool Registry & Sandboxing / Trap: dangerous capabilities granted without declaration
failures.title: Dangerous tools are available to agent without explicit capability gate
failures.problem: The harness registers high-risk tools (file deletion, database mutation, external API writes) in the same registry as safe tools with no distinction. The agent can invoke dangerous tools at any point in the loop without triggering a capability check or escalation. Destructive operations occur silently.
failures.expected: Dangerous tools (those with external or destructive side effects) require an explicit capability declaration before the harness exposes them to the agent. The capability declaration is versioned and audited. The harness refuses to start if a required capability declaration is missing.
failures.success_criteria: The tool registry or harness startup procedure includes a capabilities manifest listing which tools are gated. The harness raises an error if a gated tool is invoked without a matching capability declaration. A comment in the registry ('use with caution') does not satisfy this criterion — a machine-readable gate is required.
failures.reference_solution: Add a `capabilities.json` manifest to the harness config. Each dangerous tool entry in the registry references a required capability key. The tool runner checks the capabilities manifest before dispatching a gated tool call and raises CapabilityNotGrantedError if the key is absent.

failures.source_ref: Stage: Governance & Human Oversight / Trap: irreversible actions executed without human checkpoint
failures.title: Agent executes irreversible operations without a human-review pause
failures.problem: The harness allows the agent to perform irreversible actions (file deletion, deployment, financial transaction) in a single agentic step with no pause for human review. The operation is completed before any human can inspect or veto it. Rollback is not possible after the fact.
failures.expected: The control plane enforces a pause-and-wait mechanism for actions classified as irreversible. Before executing any action in the escalation category, the harness emits a PENDING_APPROVAL event, suspends the loop, and waits for an explicit approval signal (e.g., `/approve` command or async approval queue entry).
failures.success_criteria: The harness governance documentation enumerates at least 3 specific action categories that require human review before execution. The control plane documentation or code describes the pause-and-wait mechanism. A policy that says 'irreversible actions require human review' without specifying the action categories and the enforcement mechanism does not satisfy this criterion.
failures.reference_solution: Define an `escalation_policy.md` listing action categories: file_deletion, api_write, financial_transaction, deployment, database_mutation. Add an escalation hook in the tool runner that emits PENDING_APPROVAL, writes to an approval queue, and blocks until an approval record appears with a matching request_id.

failures.source_ref: Stage: Governance & Human Oversight / Trap: no audit trail for escalated decisions
failures.title: Escalated governance decisions have no persistent audit trail
failures.problem: The harness has a pause-and-wait mechanism for irreversible actions, but approved and rejected decisions are not logged persistently. After the sprint ends, there is no record of which actions were escalated, who approved them, or what the agent was about to do. Post-incident review is impossible.
failures.expected: Every escalated decision is appended to a persistent audit log with: (a) timestamp, (b) action category, (c) action payload (redacted if sensitive), (d) approver identity or signal, (e) decision (approved/rejected), and (f) outcome. The audit log is never truncated during normal operation.
failures.success_criteria: The harness governance documentation references a named audit log artifact (e.g., audit-log.jsonl). The log schema includes at minimum: timestamp, action_category, decision, and approver fields. A harness that logs only 'action approved' without the action payload or approver identity does not satisfy this criterion.
failures.reference_solution: Append each escalated decision to `.harness/audit-log.jsonl` as a JSONL record. Schema: `{ ts, request_id, action_category, action_summary, approver, decision, outcome }`. The harness startup procedure checks that the audit log file is writable before starting the loop.

failures.source_ref: Stage: Projection & Planning / Trap: no separate planning phase
failures.title: Agent plans inline during execution with no separate planning step
failures.problem: The harness provides the sprint goal directly to the agentic loop with no dedicated planning phase. The agent decomposes the goal implicitly during execution, mixing reasoning and action in each loop step. There is no structured plan artifact, so execution divergence cannot be detected.
failures.expected: The harness workflow includes a dedicated planning phase before the execution loop begins. The planning phase produces a structured plan artifact (ordered steps, tool assignments, estimated token budget per step). The execution loop reads the plan and advances through it step by step.
failures.success_criteria: The harness control plane documentation describes a named planning phase that precedes execution. The plan output is a structured artifact (JSON or structured markdown), not inline prose. A harness where the agent's first loop step is 'decide what to do' (rather than executing a pre-produced plan step) does not satisfy this criterion.
failures.reference_solution: Add a PLANNING state to the control plane state machine. In the PLANNING state, the harness invokes the planner skill with the sprint goal and sprint context. The planner produces a `plan.json` with an ordered array of steps. The EXECUTING state advances through plan.json one step at a time.

failures.source_ref: Stage: Projection & Planning / Trap: plan state not persisted across restarts
failures.title: Plan progress is lost when the harness is restarted mid-sprint
failures.problem: The harness produces a plan at sprint start, but plan progress (which steps are complete, which are in progress) is stored only in memory. When the harness process is restarted mid-sprint, the plan is regenerated from scratch and previously completed steps are re-executed. Idempotency is not enforced.
failures.expected: Plan progress is persisted to a file or store after each step completion. On restart, the harness reads the persisted plan state and resumes from the first incomplete step. Completed steps are not re-executed unless explicitly requested.
failures.success_criteria: The harness planning subsystem documentation references a named plan state artifact (e.g., plan.json, sprint-state.json plan_progress field). The documentation describes the resume-from-checkpoint behavior on restart. A harness whose only state persistence is a log file (no structured step completion record) does not satisfy this criterion.
failures.reference_solution: Add a `plan_progress` field to `sprint-state.json`: an object mapping each plan step ID to its status (pending / in_progress / complete / failed). On restart, read sprint-state.json, skip steps with status 'complete', and resume at the first 'pending' or 'in_progress' step.

failures.source_ref: Stage: Skills & Instruction Execution / Trap: instructions written for developer not model
failures.title: Skill instructions are written for the developer, not the executing model
failures.problem: Skill definition files contain prose notes and implementation rationale intended for human readers — not step-by-step instructions that an agent model can execute. The model cannot reliably execute the skill because the instructions describe intent rather than action. Skill invocation produces inconsistent, hallucinated, or off-scope behavior.
failures.expected: Each skill's SKILL.md contains step-by-step instructions written in imperative form for the executing model: numbered steps, explicit tool calls, defined inputs and outputs per step, and context scope annotations limiting what the model reads at each step. The instructions describe what the agent does, not what the developer intended.
failures.success_criteria: Each skill file has a frontmatter manifest (name, description, allowed-tools) AND numbered step instructions in imperative form. At least one step includes an explicit context scope annotation (e.g., a comment describing what files to read or skip). A skill file that contains only rationale prose without numbered imperative steps does not satisfy this criterion.
failures.reference_solution: Rewrite skill instructions using the ACI instruction format: Step 1 — [imperative action]. Step 2 — [imperative action]. Add frontmatter with allowed-tools. Add a context scope comment at each step that reads more than 2 files: '<!-- Context scope at this step: read only X and Y — do not read Z. -->'.

failures.source_ref: Stage: Observation & Monitoring / Trap: execution transcripts are ephemeral
failures.title: Agent execution transcripts are not captured — post-sprint review is impossible
failures.problem: The harness runs the agentic loop and records only a final pass/fail result. Individual tool calls, intermediate reasoning steps, and model outputs within the loop are not captured in a persistent transcript. When a sprint fails, the evaluator cannot determine what the agent did, which tool call went wrong, or why the agent made a particular decision.
failures.expected: The harness captures a structured execution transcript for each sprint: one record per tool invocation with timestamp, tool name, input, output, and duration. The transcript is written to a file (e.g., transcripts/sprint-NN.jsonl) and is available to the evaluator before scoring.
failures.success_criteria: The harness observation subsystem documentation describes a named transcript artifact per sprint. The transcript schema includes at minimum: timestamp, tool_name, and tool_result fields. A harness that logs only sprint-level progress markers (e.g., 'Step 3 complete') without tool-call-level detail does not satisfy this criterion.
failures.reference_solution: Emit a JSONL record to `transcripts/sprint-NN.jsonl` for each tool call: `{ ts, sprint, step, tool_name, input_summary, output_summary, duration_ms, status }`. Add a pre-eval hook that makes the transcript available to the evaluator at the start of the eval phase.

failures.source_ref: Stage: External Affordances / Trap: outbound integrations undeclared
failures.title: Harness makes outbound API calls with no declared affordance manifest
failures.problem: The harness makes outbound network calls (to LLM APIs, external data sources, notification services) during sprint execution, but these affordances are not declared in any manifest or config. The list of external integrations is unknown from the harness documentation alone. Audit and rate-limit enforcement are not possible.
failures.expected: All outbound integrations are declared in a machine-readable manifest (e.g., affordances.json or a section in harness config). Each entry specifies the endpoint, purpose, and an optional rate limit or budget. The harness startup procedure validates that all declared endpoints are reachable before the loop begins.
failures.success_criteria: The harness documentation or config references a named affordance manifest. The manifest lists at least one external integration with its endpoint and purpose. A harness whose only record of external calls is scattered inline in skill files (with no central manifest) does not satisfy this criterion.
failures.reference_solution: Create an `affordances.json` at the harness root: `{ 'integrations': [{ 'name': 'llm-api', 'endpoint': 'https://api.anthropic.com', 'purpose': 'model inference', 'rate_limit': '50/min' }] }`. Reference affordances.json in the harness README. Add a startup health-check that pings declared endpoints.
